one_liner: A 19-year-old collegiate basketball player collapses during practice and is brought to the ED after a brief episode of syncope; he has had several prior episodes of exertional lightheadedness.

stage2.hpi_extension: He reports that for the past year he has had exertional dyspnea, occasional chest tightness with sprinting, and three prior near-syncopal episodes during games. His 22-year-old cousin died suddenly of 'a heart problem' during soccer at age 16. He takes no medications and uses no supplements. He denies fever, cough, or illicit drug use.
stage2.family_history: **Paternal cousin with sudden cardiac death at age 16**; father with 'thick heart muscle'
stage2.social_history: College student, non-smoker, rare alcohol, no drugs or performance enhancers
stage2.physical_exam: Well-developed young man, alert and oriented. Cardiac: **harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border** that **increases with Valsalva and standing** and **decreases with squatting and passive leg raise**. **Sustained apical impulse**. **S4 gallop**. No carotid radiation. Bisferiens carotid pulse. Lungs clear. No edema.

lab_results.result: Transthoracic echocardiogram: **asymmetric septal hypertrophy** (septal thickness 22 mm, LV posterior wall 10 mm; ratio 2.2). **Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the anterior mitral valve leaflet** with dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction; LVOT gradient 60 mmHg at rest. EF 70% with small hyperdynamic LV cavity.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy and systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve causing dynamic LVOT obstruction are the echocardiographic hallmarks of obstructive HCM.

lab_results.result: Sinus rhythm. **Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria** with **deep narrow Q waves in the inferior and lateral leads (septal Q waves)**. Left atrial enlargement. Nonspecific ST-T changes.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Deep septal Q waves in the inferolateral leads reflect hypertrophy of the interventricular septum and are highly characteristic of HCM.

lab_results.result: **Pathogenic mutation identified in the MYH7 gene** encoding β-myosin heavy chain.
lab_results.clinical_significance: HCM is caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere proteins, most commonly β-myosin heavy chain (MYH7) and myosin-binding protein C (MYBPC3). Genetic confirmation supports family screening.

stage5.final_clue: He is restricted from competitive athletics, started on a beta-blocker, and offered an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) given his family history of sudden cardiac death.

debrief.classic_presentation: HCM classically presents in a young athlete with exertional dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or sudden cardiac death. It is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the United States. The murmur is a harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo at the LLSB that increases with decreased preload (Valsalva, standing) — a distinguishing feature from aortic stenosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: HCM is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in sarcomere proteins — most commonly β-myosin heavy chain (MYH7) and myosin-binding protein C (MYBPC3). Histology shows **myofiber disarray** and interstitial fibrosis. Asymmetric septal hypertrophy causes dynamic LVOT obstruction worsened by systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve (Venturi effect). The small, non-compliant, hypertrophied LV leads to diastolic dysfunction and predisposes to ventricular arrhythmias.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line medical therapy is a **non-dihydropyridine: beta-blocker (or verapamil)** to slow heart rate, prolong diastolic filling, and reduce outflow obstruction. **Avoid digoxin, diuretics, vasodilators (ACEi, nitrates, hydralazine), and dihydropyridine CCBs** — all decrease preload/afterload and worsen LVOT obstruction. ICD placement for high-risk patients (family history of SCD, prior syncope, severe LVH, NSVT on Holter). Septal myectomy or alcohol septal ablation for refractory symptoms.
high_yield_pearls: HCM is the **most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes** in the U.S.; inherited autosomal dominantly with variable penetrance.
HCM murmur **increases with Valsalva and standing** (decreased preload) and **decreases with squatting, handgrip, and passive leg raise** — the opposite of nearly every other systolic murmur.
Histology: **myofiber disarray** with interlacing, chaotic myocyte arrangement — the pathognomonic finding.
**Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve** creates both LVOT obstruction and secondary mitral regurgitation via a Venturi effect.
Screen all first-degree relatives with echo and EKG; consider genetic testing for sarcomere mutations (MYH7, MYBPC3).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Cardiomyopathies — Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy' section.
